you know he murmur
yes tuan say tamb itam
thy servant do know and the gate be closed
we shall have to fight
what for he ask
for our life
have no life he say
tamb itam hear cry from the girl at the door
who know say tamb itam by audacity and cunning we may even escape
there be much fear in man 's heart too
he go out think vaguely of boat and of open sea leave jim and the girl together
have not the heart to set down here such glimpse as she have give of the hour or more she pass in there wrestle with he for the possession of her happiness
whether he have any hope what he expect what he imagine it be impossible to say
he be inflexible and with the grow loneliness of his obstinacy his spirit seem to rise above the ruin of his existence
she cry fight into his ear
she could not understand
there be nothing to fight for
he be go to prove his power in another way and conquer the fatal destiny itself
he come out into the courtyard and behind he with streaming hair wild of face breathless she stagger out and lean on the side of the doorway
open the gate he order
afterwards turn to those of his man who be inside he give they leave to depart to their home
for how long tuan ask one of they timidly
for all life he say in sombre tone
hush have fall upon the town after the outburst of wail and lamentation that have sweep over the river like gust of wind from the open abode of sorrow
but rumour fly in whisper fill the heart with consternation and horrible doubt
the robber be come back bring many other with they in great ship and there would be no refuge in the land for any one
sense of utter insecurity as during an earthquake pervade the mind of man who whisper their suspicion look at each other as if in the presence of some awful portent
the sun be sink towards the forest when dain waris 's body be bring into doramin 's campong
four man carry it in cover decently with white sheet which the old mother have send out down to the gate to meet her son on his return
they lay he at doramin 's foot and the old man sit still for long time one hand on each knee look down
the frond of palm sway gently and the foliage of fruit tree stir above his head
every single man of his people be there fully armed when the old nakhoda at last raise his eye
he move they slowly over the crowd as if seek for missing face
again his chin sink on his breast
the whisper of many man mingle with the slight rustling of the leaf
the malay who have bring tamb itam and the girl to samarang be there too
not so angry as many he say to but strike with great awe and wonder at the suddenness of man 's fate which hang over their head like cloud charge with thunder
he tell that when dain waris 's body be uncover at sign of doramin 's he whom they often call the white lord 's friend be disclose lie unchanged with his eyelid little open as if about to wake
doramin lean forward little more like one look for something fall on the ground
his eye search the body from its foot to its head for the wound maybe
it be in the forehead and small and there be no word speak while one of the by stander stoop take off the silver ring from the cold stiff hand
in silence he hold it up before doramin
murmur of dismay and horror run through the crowd at the sight of that familiar token
the old nakhoda star at it and suddenly let out one great fierce cry deep from the chest roar of pain and fury as mighty as the bellow of wound bull bring great fear into man 's heart by the magnitude of his anger and his sorrow that could be plainly discern without word
there be great stillness afterwards for space while the body be be bear aside by four man
they lay it down under tree and on the instant with one long shriek all the woman of the household begin to wail together they mourn with shrill cry the sun be set and in the interval of scream lamentation the high sing song voice of two old man intone the koran chant alone
about this time jim lean on gun carriage look at the river and turn his back on the house and the girl in the doorway pant as if she have run herself to standstill be look at he across the yard
tamb itam stand not far from his master wait patiently for what might happen
all at once jim who seem to be lose in quiet thought turn to he and say time to finish this
tuan say tamb itam advance with alacrity
he do not know what his master mean but as soon as jim make movement the girl start too and walk down into the open space
it seem that no one else of the people of the house be in sight
she totter slightly and about half way down call out to jim who have apparently resume his peaceful contemplation of the river
he turn round set his back against the gun
will you fight
she cry
there be nothing to fight for he say nothing be lose
say this he make step towards she
will you fly
she cry again
there be no escape he say stop short and she stand still also silent devour he with her eye
and you shall go
she say slowly
he bend his head
she exclaim peer at he as it be you be mad or false
do you remember the night pray you to leave and you say that you could not
that it be impossible
do you remember you say you would never leave
ask you for no promise
you promise unasked remember
enough poor girl he say
should not be worth have
tamb itam say that while they be talk she would laugh loud and senselessly like one under the visitation of god
his master put his hand to his head
he be fully dress as for every day but without hat
she stop laugh suddenly
for the last time she cry menacingly will you defend yourself
nothing can touch he say in last flicker of superb egoism
tamb itam see she lean forward where she stand open her arm and run at he swiftly
she fling herself upon his breast and clasp he round the neck
but shall hold thee thus she cry
thou art mine
she sob on his shoulder
the sky over patusan be blood red immense streaming like an open vein
an enormous sun nestle crimson amongst the tree top and the forest below have black and forbid face tamb itam tell that on that evening the aspect of the heaven be angry and frightful
may well believe it for know that on that very day cyclone pass within sixty mile of the coast though there be hardly more than languid stir of air in the place
suddenly tamb itam see jim catch her arm try to unclasp her hand
she hang on they with her head fall back her hair touch the ground
come here
his master call and tamb itam help to ease she down
it be difficult to separate her finger
jim bend over she look earnestly upon her face and all at once run to the landing stage
tamb itam follow he but turn his head he see that she have struggle up to her foot
she run after they few step then fall down heavily on her knee
tuan tuan call tamb itam look back but jim be already in canoe stand up paddle in hand
he do not look back
tamb itam have just time to scramble in after he when the canoe float clear
the girl be then on her knee with clasped hand at the water gate
she remain thus for time in supplicate attitude before she spring up
you be false
she scream out after jim
forgive he cry
she call back tamb itam take the paddle from jim 's hand it be unseemly that he should sit while his lord paddle
when they reach the other shore his master forbid he to come any far but tamb itam do follow he at distance walk up the slope to doramin 's campong
it be begin to grow dark
torch twinkle here and there
those they meet seem awestruck and stand aside hastily to let jim pass
the wailing of woman come from above
the courtyard be full of armed bugis with their follower and of patusan people
do not know what this gathering really mean
be these preparation for war or for vengeance or to repulse threaten invasion
many day elapse before the people have cease to look out quake for the return of the white man with long beard and in rag whose exact relation to their own white man they could never understand
even for those simple mind poor jim remain under cloud
doramin alone
immense and desolate sit in his arm chair with the pair of flintlock pistol on his knee face by armed throng
when jim appear at somebody 's exclamation all the head turn round together and then the mass open right and leave and he walk up lane of avert glance
whisper follow he murmur he have work all the evil
he hath charm
he hear they perhaps
when he come up into the light of torch the wailing of the woman cease suddenly
doramin do not lift his head and jim stand silent before he for time
then he look to the left and move in that direction with measured step
dain waris 's mother crouch at the head of the body and the grey dishevelled hair conceal her face
jim come up slowly look at his dead friend lift the sheet than drop it without word
slowly he walk back
he come
he come be run from lip to lip make murmur to which he move
he hath take it upon his own head voice say aloud
he hear this and turn to the crowd
upon my head
few people recoil
jim wait awhile before doramin and then say gently be come in sorrow
he wait again
be come ready and unarmed he repeat
the unwieldy old man lower his big forehead like an ox under yoke make an effort to rise clutch at the flintlock pistol on his knee
from his throat come gurgle choke inhuman sound and his two attendant help he from behind
people remark that the ring which he have drop on his lap fall and roll against the foot of the white man and that poor jim glance down at the talisman that have open for he the door of fame love and success within the wall of forest fringe with white foam within the coast that under the western sun look like the very stronghold of the night
doramin struggle to keep his foot make with his two supporter sway tottering group his little eye star with an expression of mad pain of rage with ferocious glitter which the bystander notice and then while jim stand stiffen and with bared head in the light of torch look he straight in the face he cling heavily with his left arm round the neck of bow youth and lift deliberately his right shoot his son 's friend through the chest
the crowd which have fall apart behind jim as soon as doramin have raise his hand rush tumultuously forward after the shot
they say that the white man send right and leave at all those face proud and unflinching glance
then with his hand over his lip he fall forward dead
and that be the end
he pass away under cloud inscrutable at heart forget unforgiven and excessively romantic
not in the wild day of his boyish vision could he have see the alluring shape of such an extraordinary success
for it may very well be that in the short moment of his last proud and unflinching glance he have behold the face of that opportunity which like an eastern bride have come veil to his side
but we can see he an obscure conqueror of fame tear himself out of the arm of jealous love at the sign at the call of his exalted egoism
he go away from live woman to celebrate his pitiless wedding with shadowy ideal of conduct
be he satisfied quite now wonder
we ought to know
he be one of we and have not stand up once like an evoke ghost to answer for his eternal constancy
be so very wrong after all
now he be no more there be day when the reality of his existence come to with an immense with an overwhelming force and yet upon my honour there be moment too when he pass from my eye like disembodied spirit astray amongst the passion of this earth ready to surrender himself faithfully to the claim of his own world of shade
who know
he be go inscrutable at heart and the poor girl be lead sort of soundless inert life in stein 's house
stein have age greatly of late
he feel it himself and say often that he be prepare to leave all this prepare to leave
while he wave his hand sadly at his butterfly

the nigger of the narcissus tale of the forecastle by joseph conrad copyright by doubleday page company to edward garnett
this tale
about my friend of the sea to my reader in america
from that evening when james wait join the ship late for the muster of the crew to the moment when he leave we in the open sea shroud in sailcloth through the open port have much to do with he
he be in my watch
negro in british forecastle be lonely being
he have no chum
yet james wait afraid of death and make she his accomplice be an impostor of some character master our compassion scornful of our sentimentalism triumph over our suspicion
but in the book he be nothing he be merely the centre of the ship 's collective psychology and the pivot of the action
yet he who in the family circle and amongst my friend be familiarly refer to as the nigger remain very precious to
for the book write round he be not the sort of thing that can be attempt more than once in life time
it be the book by which not as novelist perhaps but as an artist strive for the utmost sincerity of expression be willing to stand or fall
its page be the tribute of my unalterable and profound affection for the ship the seaman the wind and the great sea the moulder of my youth the companion of the good year of my life
after write the last word of that book in the revulsion of feeling before the accomplished task understand that have do with the sea and that henceforth have to be writer
and almost without lay down the pen write preface try to express the spirit in which be enter on the task of my new life
that preface on advice which now think be wrong be never publish with the book
but the late w. e. henley who have the courage at that time to serialize my nigger in the new review judge it worthy to be print as an afterword at the end of the last instalment of the tale
be glad that this book which mean so much to be come out again under its proper title of the nigger of the narcissus and under the auspex of my good friend and publisher messrs. doubleday page co. into the light of publicity
half the span of generation have pass since w. e. henley after read two chapter send verbal message tell conrad that if the rest be up to the sample it shall certainly come out in the new review
the most gratifying recollection of my writer 's life
and here be the suppress preface
joseph conrad
content preface chapter one chapter two chapter three chapter four chapter five preface
work that aspire however humbly to the condition of art should carry its justification in every line
and art itself may be define as single minded attempt to render the high kind of justice to the visible universe by bring to light the truth manifold and one underlie its every aspect
it be an attempt to find in its form in its colour in its light in its shadow in the aspect of matter and in the fact of life what of each be fundamental what be endure and essential their one illuminate and convincing quality the very truth of their existence
the artist then like the thinker or the scientist seek the truth and make his appeal
impress by the aspect of the world the thinker plunge into idea the scientist into fact whence presently emerge they make their appeal to those quality of our be that fit we good for the hazardous enterprise of live
they speak authoritatively to our common sense to our intelligence to our desire of peace or to our desire of unrest not seldom to our prejudice sometimes to our fear often to our egoism but always to our credulity
and their word be hear with reverence for their concern be with weighty matter with the cultivation of our mind and the proper care of our body with the attainment of our ambition with the perfection of the mean and the glorification of our precious aim
it be otherwise with the artist
confront by the same enigmatical spectacle the artist descend within himself and in that lonely region of stress and strife if he be deserving and fortunate he find the term of his appeal
his appeal be make to our less obvious capacity to that part of our nature which because of the warlike condition of existence be necessarily keep out of sight within the more resisting and hard quality like the vulnerable body within steel armour
his appeal be less loud more profound less distinct more stirring and soon forget
yet its effect endure forever
the change wisdom of successive generation discard idea question fact demolish theory
but the artist appeal to that part of our being which be not dependent on wisdom to that in we which be gift and not an acquisition and therefore more permanently enduring
he speak to our capacity for delight and wonder to the sense of mystery surround our life to our sense of pity and beauty and pain to the latent feeling of fellowship with all creation and to the subtle but invincible conviction of solidarity that knit together the loneliness of innumerable heart to the solidarity in dream in joy in sorrow in aspiration in illusion in hope in fear which bind man to each other which bind together all humanity the dead to the living and the living to the unborn
it be only some such train of thought or rather of feeling that can in measure explain the aim of the attempt make in the tale which follow to present an unrestful episode in the obscure life of few individual out of all the disregard multitude of the bewildered the simple and the voiceless
for if any part of truth dwell in the belief confess above it become evident that there be not place of splendour or dark corner of the earth that do not deserve if only pass glance of wonder and pity
the motive then may be hold to justify the matter of the work but this preface which be simply an avowal of endeavour can not end here for the avowal be not yet complete
fiction if it at all aspire to be art appeal to temperament
and in truth it must be like painting like music like all art the appeal of one temperament to all the other innumerable temperament whose subtle and resistless power endow pass event with their true meaning and create the moral the emotional atmosphere of the place and time
such an appeal to be effective must be an impression convey through the sens and in fact it can not be make in any other way because temperament whether individual or collective be not amenable to persuasion
all art therefore appeal primarily to the sens and the artistic aim when express itself in write word must also make its appeal through the sens if its high desire be to reach the secret spring of responsive emotion
it must strenuously aspire to the plasticity of sculpture to the colour of painting and to the magic suggestiveness of music which be the art of art
and it be only through complete unswerve devotion to the perfect blending of form and substance it be only through an unremitting never discourage care for the shape and ring of sentence that an approach can be make to plasticity to colour and that the light of magic suggestiveness may be bring to play for an evanescent instant over the commonplace surface of word of the old old word wear thin deface by age of careless usage
the sincere endeavour to accomplish that creative task to go as far on that road as his strength will carry he to go undeterred by falter weariness or reproach be the only valid justification for the worker in prose
and if his conscience be clear his answer to those who in the fulness of wisdom which look for immediate profit demand specifically to be edify console amuse who demand to be promptly improve or encourage or frightened or shocked or charm must run thus:—my task which be try to achieve be by the power of the write word to make you hear to make you feel it be before all to make you see
that and no more and it be everything
if succeed you shall find there accord to your desert encouragement consolation fear charm all you demand and perhaps also that glimpse of truth for which you have forget to ask
to snatch in moment of courage from the remorseless rush of time passing phase of life be only the beginning of the task
the task approach in tenderness and faith be to hold up unquestioningly without choice and without fear the rescue fragment before all eye in the light of sincere mood
it be to show its vibration its colour its form and through its movement its form and its colour reveal the substance of its truth disclose its inspiring secret the stress and passion within the core of each convincing moment
in single minded attempt of that kind if one be deserving and fortunate one may perchance attain to such clearness of sincerity that at last the present vision of regret or pity of terror or mirth shall awaken in the heart of the beholder that feeling of unavoidable solidarity of the solidarity in mysterious origin in toil in joy in hope in uncertain fate which bind man to each other and all mankind to the visible world
it be evident that he who rightly or wrongly hold by the conviction express above can not be faithful to any one of the temporary formula of his craft
the endure part of they the truth which each only imperfectly veil should abide with he as the most precious of his possession but they all realism romanticism naturalism even the unofficial sentimentalism which like the poor be exceedingly difficult to get rid of all these god must after short period of fellowship abandon he even on the very threshold of the temple to the stammering of his conscience and to the outspoken consciousness of the difficulty of his work
in that uneasy solitude the supreme cry of art for art itself lose the exciting ring of its apparent immorality
it sound far off
it have cease to be cry and be hear only as whisper often incomprehensible but at time and faintly encouraging
sometimes stretch at ease in the shade of roadside tree we watch the motion of labourer in distant field and after time begin to wonder languidly as to what the fellow may be at
we watch the movement of his body the waving of his arm we see he bend down stand up hesitate begin again
it may add to the charm of an idle hour to be tell the purpose of his exertion
if we know he be try to lift stone to dig ditch to uproot stump we look with more real interest at his effort we be dispose to condone the jar of his agitation upon the restfulness of the landscape and even if in brotherly frame of mind we may bring ourselves to forgive his failure
we understand his object and after all the fellow have try and perhaps he have not the strength and perhaps he have not the knowledge
we forgive go on our way and forget
and so it be with the workman of art
art be long and life be short and success be very far off
and thus doubtful of strength to travel so far we talk little about the aim the aim of art which like life itself be inspiring difficult obscure by mist it be not in the clear logic of triumphant conclusion it be not in the unveiling of one of those heartless secret which be call the laws of nature
it be not less great but only more difficult
to arrest for the space of breath the hand busy about the work of the earth and compel man entrance by the sight of distant goal to glance for moment at the surround vision of form and colour of sunshine and shadow to make they pause for look for sigh for smile such be the aim difficult and evanescent and reserve only for very few to achieve
but sometimes by the deserving and the fortunate even that task be accomplish
and when it be accomplish behold!—all the truth of life be there moment of vision sigh smile and the return to an eternal rest
j. c. the nigger of the narcissus chapter one mr. baker chief mate of the ship narcissus step in one stride out of his light cabin into the darkness of the quarter deck
above his head on the break of the poop the night watchman ring double stroke
it be nine o'clock

the man limp down the ladder then say reflectively think so sir

the main deck be dark aft but halfway from forward through the open door of the forecastle two streak of brilliant light cut the shadow of the quiet night that lie upon the ship
hum of voice be hear there while port and starboard in the illuminated doorway silhouette of move man appear for moment very black without relief like figure cut out of sheet tin
the ship be ready for sea
the carpenter have drive in the last wedge of the mainhatch batten and throw down his maul have wipe his face with great deliberation just on the stroke of five
the deck have be sweep the windlass oil and make ready to heave up the anchor the big tow rope lie in long bight along one side of the main deck with one end carry up and hang over the bow in readiness for the tug that would come paddle and hiss noisily hot and smoky in the limpid cool quietness of the early morning
the captain be ashore where he have be engage some new hand to make up his full crew and the work of the day over the ship 's officer have keep out of the way glad of little breathing time
soon after dark the few liberty man and the new hand begin to arrive in shore boat row by white clothe asiatics who clamour fiercely for payment before come alongside the gangway ladder
the feverish and shrill babble of eastern language struggle against the masterful tone of tipsy seaman who argue against brazen claim and dishonest hope by profane shout
the resplendent and bestarred peace of the east be tear into squalid tatter by howl of rage and shriek of lament raise over sum range from five anna to half rupee and every soul afloat in bombay harbour become aware that the new hand be join the narcissus
gradually the distract noise have subside
the boat come no long in splash cluster of three or four together but drop alongside singly in subdue buzz of expostulation cut short by not pace more
you go to the devil

russian finn wear yellow shirt with pink stripe star upwards dreamy eyed from under mop of tumble hair
two young giant with smooth baby face two scandinavians help each other to spread their bedding silent and smile placidly at the tempest of good humoured and meaningless curse
old singleton the old able seaman in the ship sit apart on the deck right under the lamp strip to the waist tattoo like cannibal chief all over his powerful chest and enormous bicep between the blue and red pattern his white skin gleam like satin his bare back be prop against the heel of the bowsprit and he hold book at arm 's length before his big sunburnt face
with his spectacle and venerable white beard he resemble learned and savage patriarch the incarnation of barbarian wisdom serene in the blasphemous turmoil of the world
he be intensely absorb and as he turn the page an expression of grave surprise would pass over his rugged feature
he be read pelham
the popularity of bulwer lytton in the forecastle of southern go ship be wonderful and bizarre phenomenon
what idea do his polished and so curiously insincere sentence awaken in the simple mind of the big child who people those dark and wander place of the earth
what meaning can their rough inexperienced soul find in the elegant verbiage of his page
what excitement?—what forgetfulness?—what appeasement
mystery be it the fascination of the incomprehensible?—i it the charm of the impossible
or be those being who exist beyond the pale of life stir by his tale as by an enigmatical disclosure of resplendent world that exist within the frontier of infamy and filth within that border of dirt and hunger of misery and dissipation that come down on all side to the water 's edge of the incorruptible ocean and be the only thing they know of life the only thing they see of surround land those life long prisoner of the sea
singleton who have sail to the southward since the age of twelve who in the last forty five year have live as we have calculate from his paper no more than forty month ashore old singleton who boast with the mild composure of long year well spend that generally from the day he be pay off from one ship till the day he ship in another he seldom be in condition to distinguish daylight old singleton sit unmoved in the clash of voice and cry spell through pelham with slow labour and lose in an absorption profound enough to resemble trance
he breathe regularly
every time he turn the book in his enormous and blacken hand the muscle of his big white arm roll slightly under the smooth skin
hide by the white moustache his lip stain with tobacco juice that trickle down the long beard move in inward whisper
his bleared eye gaze fixedly from behind the glitter of black rimmed glass
opposite to he and on level with his face the ship 's cat sit on the barrel of the windlass in the pose of crouch chimera blink its green eye at its old friend
it seem to meditate leap on to the old man 's lap over the bent back of the ordinary seaman who sit at singleton 's foot
young charley be lean and long necked

he stand repulsive and smile in the sudden silence
this clean white forecastle be his refuge the place where he could be lazy where he could wallow and lie and eat and curse the food he eat where he could display his talent for shirk work for cheating for cadge where he could find surely some one to wheedle and some one to bully and where he would be pay for do all this

he be the man that can not steer that can not splice that dodge the work on dark night that aloft hold on frantically with both arm and leg and swear at the wind the sleet the darkness the man who curse the sea while other work

singleton stand at the door with his face to the light and his back to the darkness
and alone in the dim emptiness of the sleep forecastle he appear big colossal very old old as father time himself who should have come there into this place as quiet as sepulchre to contemplate with patient eye the short victory of sleep the consoler
yet he be only child of time lonely relic of devour and forget generation
he stand still strong as ever unthinking ready man with vast empty past and with no future with his childlike impulse and his man 's passion already dead within his tattooed breast
the man who could understand his silence be go those man who know how to exist beyond the pale of life and within sight of eternity
they have be strong as those be strong who know neither doubt nor hope
they have be impatient and enduring turbulent and devoted unruly and faithful
well mean people have try to represent those man as whine over every mouthful of their food as go about their work in fear of their life
but in truth they have be man who know toil privation violence debauchery but know not fear and have no desire of spite in their heart
man hard to manage but easy to inspire voiceless man but man enough to scorn in their heart the sentimental voice that bewail the hardness of their fate
it be fate unique and their own the capacity to bear it appear to they the privilege of the choose
their generation live inarticulate and indispensable without know the sweetness of affection or the refuge of home and die free from the dark menace of narrow grave
they be the everlasting child of the mysterious sea
their successor be the grow up child of discontented earth
they be less naughty but less innocent less profane but perhaps also less believe and if they have learn how to speak they have also learn how to whine
but the other be strong and mute they be efface bow and endure like stone caryatide that hold up in the night the light hall of resplendent and glorious edifice
they be go now and it do not matter
the sea and the earth be unfaithful to their child truth faith generation of man go and be forget and it do not matter
except perhaps to the few of those who believe the truth confess the faith or love the man
breeze be come
the ship that have be lie tide ride swing to heavy puff and suddenly the slack of the chain cable between the windlass and the hawse pipe clinked slip forward an inch and rise gently off the deck with startling suggestion as of unsuspected life that have be lurk stealthily in the iron
in the hawse pipe the grind link send through the ship sound like low groan of man sigh under burden
the strain come on the windlass the chain tauten like string vibrate and the handle of the screw brake move in slight jerk
singleton step forward
till then he have be stand meditative and unthinking reposeful and hopeless with face grim and blank sixty year old child of the mysterious sea
the thought of all his lifetime could have be express in six word but the stir of those thing that be as much part of his existence as his beat heart call up gleam of alert understanding upon the sternness of his aged face
the flame of the lamp sway and the old man with knitted and bushy eyebrow stand over the brake watchful and motionless in the wild saraband of dance shadow
then the ship obedient to the call of her anchor forge ahead slightly and ease the strain
the cable relieve hang down and after sway imperceptibly to and fro drop with loud tap on the hard wood plank
singleton seize the high lever and by violent throw forward of his body wring out another half turn from the brake
he recover himself breathe largely and remain for while glare down at the powerful and compact engine that squat on the deck at his foot like some quiet monster creature amazing and tame
you hold
he growl at it masterfully in the incult tangle of his white beard chapter two next morning at daylight the narcissus go to sea
slight haze blur the horizon
outside the harbour the measureless expanse of smooth water lie sparkling like floor of jewel and as empty as the sky
the short black tug give pluck to windward in the usual way then let go the rope and hover for moment on the quarter with her engine stop while the slim long hull of the ship move ahead slowly under low topsail
the loose upper canvas blow out in the breeze with soft round contour resemble small white cloud snare in the maze of rope
then the sheet be haul home the yard hoist and the ship become high and lonely pyramid gliding all shin and white through the sunlit mist
the tug turn short round and go away towards the land
twenty six pair of eye watch her low broad stern crawl languidly over the smooth swell between the two paddle wheel that turn fast beat the water with fierce hurry
she resemble an enormous and aquatic black beetle surprise by the light overwhelm by the sunshine try to escape with ineffectual effort into the distant gloom of the land
she leave linger smudge of smoke on the sky and two vanish trail of foam on the water
on the place where she have stop round black patch of soot remain undulate on the swell an unclean mark of the creature 's rest
the narcissus leave alone head south seem to stand resplendent and still upon the restless sea under the move sun
flake of foam sweep past her side the water strike she with flash blow the land glide away slowly fade few bird scream on motionless wing over the sway masthead
but soon the land disappear the bird go away and to the west the pointed sail of an arab dhow run for bombay rise triangular and upright above the sharp edge of the horizon linger and vanish like an illusion
then the ship 's wake long and straight stretch itself out through day of immense solitude
the set sun burn on the level of the water flame crimson below the blackness of heavy rain cloud
the sunset squall come up from behind dissolve itself into the short deluge of hiss shower
it leave the ship glisten from truck to water line and with darkened sail
she run easily before fair monsoon with her deck clear for the night and move along with she be hear the sustained and monotonous swishing of the wave mingle with the low whisper of man muster aft for the setting of watch the short plaint of some block aloft or now and then loud sigh of wind
mr. baker come out of his cabin call out the first name sharply before close the door behind he
he be go to take charge of the deck
on the homeward trip accord to an old custom of the sea the chief officer take the first night watch from eight till midnight
so mr. baker after he have hear the last yes sir say moodily relieve the wheel and look out and climb with heavy foot the poop ladder to windward
soon after mr. creighton come down whistle softly and go into the cabin
on the doorstep the steward lounge in slipper meditative and with his shirt sleeve roll up to the armpit
on the main deck the cook lock up the galley door have an altercation with young charley about pair of sock

aft on the high poop mr. baker walk shuffle and grunt to himself in the pause of his thought
forward the look out man erect between the fluke of the two anchor hum an endless tune keep his eye fix dutifully ahead in vacant stare
multitude of star come out into the clear night people the emptiness of the sky
they glitter as if alive above the sea they surround the run ship on all side more intense than the eye of star crowd and as inscrutable as the soul of man
the passage have begin and the ship fragment detach from the earth go on lonely and swift like small planet
round she the abyss of sky and sea meet in an unattainable frontier
great circular solitude move with she ever change and ever the same always monotonous and always impose
now and then another wander white speck burden with life appear far off disappear intent on its own destiny
the sun look upon she all day and every morning rise with burning round stare of undying curiosity
she have her own future she be alive with the life of those being who tread her deck like that earth which have give she up to the sea she have an intolerable load of regret and hope
on she live timid truth and audacious lie and like the earth she be unconscious fair to see and condemn by man to an ignoble fate
the august loneliness of her path lend dignity to the sordid inspiration of her pilgrimage
she drive foam to the southward as if guide by the courage of high endeavour
the smile greatness of the sea dwarf the extent of time
the day race after one another brilliant and quick like the flash of lighthouse and the night eventful and short resemble fleeting dream
the man have shake into their place and the half hourly voice of the bell rule their life of unceasing care
night and day the head and shoulder of seaman could be see aft by the wheel outline high against sunshine or starlight very steady above the stir of revolving spoke
the face change pass in rotation
youthful face bearded face dark face face serene or face moody but all akin with the brotherhood of the sea all with the same attentive expression of eye carefully watch the compass or the sail
captain allistoun serious and with an old red muffler round his throat all day long pervade the poop
at night many time he rise out of the darkness of the companion such as phantom above grave and stand watchful and mute under the star his night shirt flutter like flag then without sound sink down again
he be bear on the shore of the pentland firth
in his youth he attain the rank of harpooner in peterhead whaler
when he speak of that time his restless grey eye become still and cold like the loom of ice
afterwards he go into the east indian trade for the sake of change
he have command the narcissus since she be build
he love his ship and drive she unmercifully for his secret ambition be to make she accomplish some day brilliantly quick passage which would be mention in nautical paper
he pronounce his owner 's name with sardonic smile speak but seldom to his officer and reprove error in gentle voice with word that cut to the quick
his hair be iron grey his face hard and of the colour of pump leather
he shave every morning of his life at six but once be catch in fierce hurricane eighty mile southwest of mauritius he have miss three consecutive day
he fear naught but an unforgiving god and wish to end his day in little house with plot of ground attach far in the country out of sight of the sea
he the ruler of that minute world seldom descend from the olympian height of his poop
below he at his foot so to speak common mortal lead their busy and insignificant life
along the main deck mr. baker grunt in manner bloodthirsty and innocuous and keep all our nose to the grindstone be as he once remark pay for do that very thing
the man work about the deck be healthy and contented as most seaman be when once well out to sea
the true peace of god begin at any spot thousand mile from the near land and when he send there the messenger of his might it be not in terrible wrath against crime presumption and folly but paternally to chasten simple heart ignorant heart that know nothing of life and beat undisturbed by envy or greed
in the evening the clear deck have reposeful aspect resemble the autumn of the earth
the sun be sink to rest wrap in mantle of warm cloud

some show chuckle exultation

wamibo after long period of star dreaminess attempt abortive smile and one of the young scandinavians much torment by doubt venture in the second dog watch to approach singleton the old man do not encourage we much to speak to he and ask sheepishly:—“you think he will die
singleton look up.—“why of course he will die he say deliberately
this seem decisive
it be promptly impart to every one by he who have consult the oracle
shy and eager he would step up and with avert gaze recite his formula:—“old singleton say he will die
it be relief
at last we know that our compassion would not be misplace and we could again smile without misgiving but we reckon without donkin
donkin do not want to ave no truck with them dirty furriner
when nilsen come to he with the news singleton say he will die he answer he by spiteful and so will you you fat head dutchman
wish you dutchmen be all dead—'stead comin takin our money inter your starvin country
we be appalled
we perceive that after all singleton 's answer mean nothing
we begin to hate he for make fun of we
all our certitude be go we be on doubtful term with our officer the cook have give we up for lose we have overhear the boatswain 's opinion that we be crowd of softy
we suspect jimmy one another and even our very self
we do not know what to do
at every insignificant turn of our humble life we meet jimmy overbearing and block the way arm in arm with his awful and veil familiar
it be weird servitude
it begin week after leave bombay and come on we stealthily like any other great misfortune
every one have remark that jimmy from the first be very slack at his work but we think it simply the outcome of his philosophy of life
donkin said:—“you put no more weight on rope than bloody sparrer
he disdain he
belfast ready for fight exclaim provokingly:—“you do not kill yourself old man!”—“would you
he retort with extreme scorn and belfast retire
one morning as we be washing deck mr. baker call to him:—“bre your broom over here wait
he stroll languidly
move yourself
ough grunt mr. baker what be the matter with your hind leg
he stop dead short
he gaze slowly with eye that bulge out with an expression audacious and sad.—“it be not my leg he say it be my lung
everybody listened.—“what be
what be wrong with they inquire mr. baker
all the watch stand around on the wet deck grin and with broom or bucket in their hand
he say mournfully:—“goe or go
can not you see be die man
know it
mr. baker be disgusted.—“then why the devil do you ship aboard here?”—“i must live till die mustn't
he reply
the grin become audible.—“go off my deck get out of my sight say mr. baker
he be nonplussed
it be unique experience
james wait obedient drop his broom and walk slowly forward
burst of laughter follow he
it be too funny
all hand laugh
they laugh
alas he become the tormentor of all our moment he be bad than nightmare
you could not see that there be anything wrong with he nigger do not show
he be not very fat certainly but then he be no lean than other nigger we have know
he cough often but the most prejudiced person could perceive that mostly he cough when it suit his purpose
he would not or could not do his work and he would not lie up
one day he would skip aloft with the good of they and next time we would be oblige to risk our life to get his limp body down
he be report he be examine he be remonstrate with threaten cajole lecture
he be call into the cabin to interview the captain
there be wild rumour
it be say he have cheek the old man it be say he have frighten he
charley maintain that the skipper weepin as giv be blessin an pot of jam
knowles have it from the steward that the unspeakable jimmy have be reel against the cabin furniture that he have groan that he have complain of general brutality and disbelief and have end by cough all over the old man 's meteorological journal which be then spread on the table
at any rate wait return forward support by the steward who in pained and shocked voice entreat us:—“here
catch hold of he one of you
he be to lie up
jimmy drink tin mugful of coffee and after bully first one and then another go to bed
he remain there most of the time but when it suit he would come on deck and appear amongst we
he be scornful and brooding he look ahead upon the sea and no one could tell what be the meaning of that black man sit apart in meditative attitude and as motionless as carving
he refuse steadily all medicine he throw sago and cornflour overboard till the steward get tired of bring it to he
he ask for paregoric
they send he big bottle enough to poison wilderness of baby
he keep it between his mattress and the deal lining of the ship 's side and nobody ever see he take dose
donkin abuse he to his face jeer at he while he gasp and the same day wait would lend he warm jersey
once donkin revile he for half an hour reproach he with the extra work his malingering give to the watch and end by call he black face swine
under the spell of our accursed perversity we be horror strike
but jimmy positively seem to revel in that abuse
it make he look cheerful and donkin have pair of old sea boot throw at he
here you east end trash boom wait you may have that
at last mr. baker have to tell the captain that james wait be disturb the peace of the ship
knock discipline on the head he will ough grunt mr. baker
as matter of fact the starboard watch come as near as possible to refuse duty when order one morning by the boatswain to wash out their forecastle
it appear jimmy object to wet floor and that morning we be in compassionate mood
we think the boatswain brute and practically tell he so
only mr. baker 's delicate tact prevent an all fire row he refuse to take we seriously
he come bustle forward and call we many unpolite name but in such hearty and seamanlike manner that we begin to feel ashamed of ourselves
in truth we think he much too good sailor to annoy he willingly and after all jimmy might have be fraud probably be
the forecastle get clean up that morning but in the afternoon sick bay be fit up in the deck house
it be nice little cabin opening on deck and with two berth
jimmy 's belonging be transport there and then notwithstanding his protest jimmy himself
he say he could not walk
four man carry he on blanket
he complain that he would have to die there alone like dog
we grieve for he and be delighted to have he remove from the forecastle
we attend he as before
the galley be next door and the cook look in many time day
wait become little more cheerful
knowles affirm having hear he laugh to himself in peal one day
other have see he walk about on deck at night
his little place with the door ajar on long hook be always full of tobacco smoke
we speak through the crack cheerfully sometimes abusively as we pass by intent on our work
he fascinate we
he would never let doubt die
he overshadow the ship
invulnerable in his promise of speedy corruption he trample on our self respect he demonstrate to we daily our want of moral courage he taint our life
have we be miserable gang of wretched immortal unhallowed alike by hope and fear he could not have lord it over we with more pitiless assertion of his sublime privilege
chapter three
meantime the narcissus with square yard run out of the fair monsoon
she drift slowly swing round and round the compass through few day of baffling light air
under the patter of short warm shower grumble man whirl the heavy yard from side to side they catch hold of the soak rope with groan and sigh while their officer sulky and dripping with rain water unceasingly order they about in weary voice
during the short respite they look with disgust into the smart palm of their stiff hand and ask one another bitterly:—“who would be sailor if he could be farmer
all the temper be spoil and no man care what he say
one black night when the watch pant in the heat and half drown with the rain have be through four mortal hour hunt from brace to brace belfast declare that he would chuck the sea for ever and go in steamer
this be excessive no doubt
captain allistoun with great self control would mutter sadly to mr. baker:—“it be not so bad not so bad when he have manage to shove and dodge and manoeuvre his smart ship through sixty mile in twenty four hour
from the doorstep of the little cabin jimmy chin in hand watch our distasteful labour with insolent and melancholy eye
we speak to he gently and out of his sight exchange sour smile
then again with fair wind and under clear sky the ship go on pile up the south latitude
she pass outside madagascar and mauritius without glimpse of the land
extra lashing be put on the spare spar
hatch be look to
the steward in his leisure moment and with worried air try to fit washboard to the cabin door
stout canvas be bent with care
anxious eye look to the westward towards the cape of storm
the ship begin to dip into southwest swell and the softly luminous sky of low latitude take on hard sheen from day to day above our head it arch high above the ship vibrate and pale like an immense dome of steel resonant with the deep voice of freshen gale
the sunshine gleam cold on the white curl of black wave
before the strong breath of westerly squall the ship with reduced sail lie slowly over obstinate and yielding
she drive to and fro in the unceasing endeavour to fight her way through the invisible violence of the wind she pitch headlong into dark smooth hollow she struggle upwards over the snowy ridge of great run sea she roll restless from side to side like thing in pain
enduring and valiant she answer to the call of man and her slim spar wave for ever in abrupt semicircle seem to beckon in vain for help towards the stormy sky
it be bad winter off the cape that year
the relieved helmsman come off flap their arm or run stamp hard and blow into swollen red finger
the watch on deck dodge the sting of cold spray or crouch in sheltered corner watch dismally the high and merciless sea board the ship time after time in unappeasable fury
water tumble in cataract over the forecastle door
you have to dash through waterfall to get into your damp bed
the man turn in wet and turn out stiff to face the redeeming and ruthless exaction of their glorious and obscure fate
far aft and peer watchfully to windward the officer could be see through the mist of squall
they stand by the weather rail hold on grimly straight and glisten in their long coat and in the disordered plunge of the hard drive ship they appear high up attentive toss violently above the grey line of clouded horizon in motionless attitude
they watch the weather and the ship as man on shore watch the momentous chance of fortune
captain allistoun never leave the deck as though he have be part of the ship 's fitting
now and then the steward shiver but always in shirt sleeve would struggle towards he with some hot coffee half of which the gale blow out of the cup before it reach the master 's lip
he drink what be leave gravely in one long gulp while heavy spray patter loudly on his oilskin coat the sea swish break about his high boot and he never take his eye off the ship
he keep his gaze rivet upon she as love man watch the unselfish toil of delicate woman upon the slender thread of whose existence be hang the whole meaning and joy of the world
we all watch she
she be beautiful and have weakness
we love she no less for that
we admire her quality aloud we boast of they to one another as though they have be our own and the consciousness of her only fault we keep bury in the silence of our profound affection
she be bear in the thunder peal of hammer beat upon iron in black eddy of smoke under grey sky on the bank of the clyde
the clamorous and sombre stream give birth to thing of beauty that float away into the sunshine of the world to be love by man
the narcissus be one of that perfect brood
less perfect than many perhaps but she be ours and consequently incomparable
we be proud of she
in bombay ignorant landlubber allude to she as that pretty grey ship
scurvy meed of commendation
we know she be the most magnificent sea boat ever launch
we try to forget that like many good sea boat she be at time rather crank
she be exact
she want care in loading and handling and no one know exactly how much care would be enough
such be the imperfection of mere man
the ship know and sometimes would correct the presumptuous human ignorance by the wholesome discipline of fear
we have hear ominous story about past voyage
the cook technically seaman but in reality no sailor)—the cook when unstrung by some misfortune such as the rolling over of saucepan would mutter gloomily while he wipe the floor:—“there
look at what she have do
some voy'ge she will drown all hand
you will see if she will not
to which the steward snatch in the galley moment to draw breath in the hurry of his worried life would remark philosophically:—“those that see will not tell anyhow
do not want to see it
we deride those fear
our heart go out to the old man when he press she hard so as to make she hold she own hold to every inch gain to windward when he make she under reefed sail leap obliquely at enormous wave
the man knit together aft into ready group by the first sharp order of an officer come to take charge of the deck in bad weather:—“keep handy the watch stand admire her valiance
their eye blink in the wind their dark face be wet with drop of water more salt and bitter than human tear beard and moustache soak hang straight and dripping like fine seaweed
they be fantastically misshapen in high boot in hat like helmet and sway clumsily stiff and bulky in glisten oilskin they resemble man strangely equip for some fabulous adventure
whenever she rise easily to tower green sea elbow dig rib face brighten lip murmured:—“didn't she do it cleverly and all the head turn like one watch with sardonic grin the foil wave go roar to leeward white with the foam of monstrous rage
but when she have not be quick enough and strike heavily lie over tremble under the blow we clutch at rope and look up at the narrow band of drenched and strain sail wave desperately aloft we think in our hearts:—“no wonder
poor thing
the thirty second day out of bombay begin inauspiciously
in the morning sea smash one of the galley door
we dash in through lot of steam and find the cook very wet and indignant with the ship:—“she 's get bad every day
she be try to drown in front of my own stove
he be very angry
we pacify he and the carpenter though wash away twice from there manage to repair the door
through that accident our dinner be not ready till late but it do not matter in the end because knowles who go to fetch it get knock down by sea and the dinner go over the side
captain allistoun look more hard and thin lipped than ever hang on to full topsail and foresail and would not notice that the ship ask to do too much appear to lose heart altogether for the first time since we know she
she refuse to rise and bore her way sullenly through the sea
twice run as though she have be blind or weary of life she put her nose deliberately into big wave and sweep the deck from end to end
as the boatswain observe with marked annoyance while we be splash about in body to try and save worthless wash tub:—“every bloom thing in the ship be go overboard this afternoon
venerable singleton break his habitual silence and say with glance aloft:—“the old man be in temper with the weather but it be no good bein angry with the wind of heaven
jimmy have shut his door of course
we know he be dry and comfortable within his little cabin and in our absurd way be please one moment exasperate the next by that certitude
donkin skulk shamelessly uneasy and miserable
he grumbled:—“i'm perishin with cold outside in bloomin wet rag an that ere black sojer sit dry on blame chest full of bloomin clothe blank his black soul
we take no notice of he we hardly give thought to jimmy and his bosom friend
there be no leisure for idle probing of heart
sail blow adrift
thing break loose
cold and wet we be wash about the deck while try to repair damage
the ship toss about shake furiously like toy in the hand of lunatic
just at sunset there be rush to shorten sail before the menace of sombre hail cloud
the hard gust of wind come brutal like the blow of fist
the ship relieve of her canvas in time receive it pluckily she yield reluctantly to the violent onset then come up with stately and irresistible motion bring her spar to windward in the tooth of the screech squall
out of the abysmal darkness of the black cloud overhead white hail stream on she rattle on the rigging leap in handful off the yard rebound on the deck round and gleam in the murky turmoil like shower of pearl
it pass away
for moment livid sun shoot horizontally the last ray of sinister light between the hill of steep rolling wave
then wild night rush in stamp out in great howl that dismal remnant of stormy day
there be no sleep on board that night
most seaman remember in their life one or two such night of culminate gale
nothing seem leave of the whole universe but darkness clamour fury and the ship
and like the last vestige of shatter creation she drift bear an anguished remnant of sinful mankind through the distress tumult and pain of an avenge terror
no one sleep in the forecastle
the tin oil lamp suspend on long string smoking describe wide circle wet clothing make dark heap on the glisten floor thin layer of water rush to and fro
in the bed place man lie boot rest on elbow and with open eye
hung up suit of oilskin swing out and in lively and disquieting like reckless ghost of decapitated seaman dance in tempest
no one speak and all listen
outside the night moan and sob to the accompaniment of continuous loud tremor as of innumerable drum beat far off
shriek pass through the air
tremendous dull blow make the ship tremble while she roll under the weight of the sea topple on her deck
at time she soar up swiftly as if to leave this earth for ever then during interminable moment fall through void with all the heart on board of her stand still till frightful shock expect and sudden start they off again with big thump
after every dislocate jerk of the ship wamibo stretch full length his face on the pillow groan slightly with the pain of his torment universe
now and then for the fraction of an intolerable second the ship in the fierce burst of terrible uproar remain on her side vibrate and still with stillness more appalling than the wild motion
then upon all those prone body stir would pass shiver of suspense
man would protrude his anxious head and pair of eye glisten in the sway of light glare wildly
some move their leg little as if make ready to jump out
but several motionless on their back and with one hand grip hard the edge of the bunk smoke nervously with quick puff star upwards immobilise in great craving for peace
at midnight order be give to furl the fore and mizen topsail
with immense effort man crawl aloft through merciless buffeting save the canvas and crawl down almost exhausted to bear in panting silence the cruel battering of the sea
perhaps for the first time in the history of the merchant service the watch tell to go below do not leave the deck as if compel to remain there by the fascination of venomous violence
at every heavy gust man huddle together whisper to one another,—“it can blow no harder,”—and presently the gale would give they the lie with piercing shriek and drive their breath back into their throat
fierce squall seem to burst asunder the thick mass of sooty vapour and above the wrack of tear cloud glimpse could be catch of the high moon rush backwards with frightful speed over the sky right into the wind 's eye
many hang their head mutter that it turn their inward out to look at it
soon the cloud close up and the world again become raging blind darkness that howl fling at the lonely ship salt spray and sleet
about half past seven the pitchy obscurity round we turn ghastly grey and we know that the sun have rise
this unnatural and threaten daylight in which we could see one another 's wild eye and draw face be only an add tax on our endurance
the horizon seem to have come on all side within arm 's length of the ship
into that narrow circle furious sea leap in strike and leap out
rain of salt heavy drop fly aslant like mist
the main topsail have to be goose winged and with stolid resignation every one prepare to go aloft once more but the officer yell push back and at last we understand that no more man would be allow to go on the yard than be absolutely necessary for the work
as at any moment the mast be likely to be jump out or blow overboard we conclude that the captain do not want to see all his crowd go over the side at once
that be reasonable
the watch then on duty lead by mr. creighton begin to struggle up the rigging
the wind flatten they against the ratline then ease little would let they ascend couple of step and again with sudden gust pin all up the shroud the whole crawl line in attitude of crucifixion
the other watch plunge down on the main deck to haul up the sail
man 's head bob up as the water fling they irresistibly from side to side
mr. baker grunt encouragingly in our midst splutter and blow amongst the tangle rope like an energetic porpoise
favour by an ominous and untrustworthy lull the work be do without any one be lose either off the deck or from the yard
for the moment the gale seem to take off and the ship as if grateful for our effort pluck up heart and make well weather of it
at eight the man off duty watch their chance run forward over the flood deck to get some rest
the other half of the crew remain aft for their turn of see she through her trouble as they express it
the two mate urge the master to go below
mr. baker grunt in his ear:—“ough
surely now ough
confidence in we
nothing more to do
she must lay it out or go
tall young mr. creighton smile down at he cheerfully
she be as right as trivet
take spell sir
he look at they stonily with bloodshot sleepless eye
the rim of his eyelid be scarlet and he move his jaw unceasingly with slow effort as though he have be masticate lump of india rubber
he shake his head
he repeated:—“never mind
must see it out must see it out but he consent to sit down for moment on the skylight with his hard face turn unflinchingly to windward
the sea spat at it and stoical it stream with water as though he have be weep
on the weather side of the poop the watch hang on to the mizen rigging and to one another try to exchange encouraging word
singleton at the wheel yell out:—“look out for yourself
his voice reach they in warning whisper
they be startled
big foam sea come out of the mist it make for the ship roar wildly and in its rush it look as mischievous and discompose as madman with an axe
one or two shout scramble up the rigging most with convulsive catch of the breath hold on where they stand
singleton dig his knee under the wheel box and carefully ease the helm to the headlong pitch of the ship but without take his eye off the come wave
it tower close to and high like wall of green glass top with snow
the ship rise to it as though she have soar on wing and for moment rest poise upon the foam crest as if she have be great sea bird
before we could draw breath heavy gust strike she another roller take she unfairly under the weather bow she give topple lurch and fill her deck
captain allistoun leap up and fall archie roll over he screaming:—“she will rise
she give another lurch to leeward the low deadeye dip heavily the man 's foot fly from under they and they hang kick above the slant poop
they could see the ship put her side in the water and shout all together:—“she 's go
forward the forecastle door fly open and the watch below be see leap out one after another throw their arm up and fall on hand and knee scramble aft on all four along the high side of the deck slop more than the roof of house
from leeward the sea rise pursue they they look wretched in hopeless struggle like vermin flee before flood they fight up the weather ladder of the poop one after another half naked and star wildly and as soon as they get up they shoot to leeward in cluster with closed eye till they bring up heavily with their rib against the iron stanchion of the rail then groan they roll in confused mass
the immense volume of water throw forward by the last scend of the ship have burst the lee door of the forecastle
they could see their chest pillow blanket clothing come out float upon the sea
while they struggle back to windward they look in dismay
the straw bed swim high the blanket spread out undulate while the chest waterlogge and with heavy list pitch heavily like dismaste hulk before they sink archie 's big coat pass with outspread arm resemble drown seaman float with his head under water
man be slip down while try to dig their finger into the plank other jam in corner roll enormous eye
they all yell unceasingly:—“the mast
black squall howl low over the ship that lie on her side with the weather yard arm pointing to the cloud while the tall mast incline nearly to the horizon seem to be of an immeasurable length
the carpenter let go his hold roll against the skylight and begin to crawl to the cabin entrance where big axe be keep ready for just such an emergency
at that moment the topsail sheet part the end of the heavy chain racket aloft and spark of red fire stream down through the fly spray
the sail flap once with jerk that seem to tear our heart out through our tooth and instantly change into bunch of fluttering narrow ribbon that tie themselves into knot and become quiet along the yard
captain allistoun struggle manage to stand up with his face near the deck upon which man swing on the end of rope like nest robber upon cliff
one of his foot be on somebody 's chest his face be purple his lip move

mr. baker one leg over the binnacle stand roar out:—“did you say no
not cut

no no

then all become still
they wait for the ship to turn over altogether and shake they out into the sea and upon the terrific noise of wind and sea not murmur of remonstrance come out from those man who each would have give ever so many year of life to see they damned stick go overboard
they all believe it their only chance but little hard face man shake his grey head and shout no without give they as much as glance
they be silent and gasp
they grip rail they have wind ropes'-end under their arm they clutch ringbolt they crawl in heap where there be foothold they hold on with both arm hook themselves to anything to windward with elbow with chin almost with their tooth and some unable to crawl away from where they have be fling feel the sea leap up strike against their back as they struggle upwards
singleton have stick to the wheel
his hair fly out in the wind the gale seem to take its life long adversary by the beard and shake his old head
he would not let go and with his knee force between the spoke fly up and down like man on bough

two man lower themselves cautiously to he other haul on the rope
they catch he up shove he into safe place hold he

cut some of you

it be the steward

the cook embrace wooden stanchion unconsciously repeat prayer

soon he also become silent
in all that crowd of cold and hungry man wait wearily for violent death not voice be hear they be mute and in sombre thoughtfulness listen to the horrible imprecation of the gale
hour pass
they be shelter by the heavy inclination of the ship from the wind that rush in one long unbroken moan above their head but cold rain shower fall at time into the uneasy calm of their refuge
under the torment of that new infliction pair of shoulder would writhe little
tooth chatter
the sky be clear and bright sunshine gleam over the ship

between two bearded shellback charley fasten with somebody 's long muffler to deck ring bolt weep quietly with rare tear wring out by bewilderment cold hunger and general misery

turn about with prudence he work himself out of his coat and throw it over the boy

they fling their arm over and press against he

mr. creighton who have hurt his leg lie amongst we with compressed lip

other unable to discern such fine shade of refinement respect he for his smartness

before his eye the ship with half her deck below water rise and fall on heavy sea that rush from under her flash in the cold sunshine

face brighten

they recall the aspect of forget shipmate and hear the voice of dead and go skipper
they remember the noise of gaslit street the steamy heat of tap room or the scorching sunshine of calm day at sea

some man fancy they have hear he exchange murmur with the master but at that time the memory be incomparably more vivid than anything actual and they be not certain whether the murmur be hear now or many year ago
they do not try to find out
mutter more or less do not matter
it be too cold for curiosity and almost for hope
they could not spare moment or thought from the great mental occupation of wish to live
and the desire of life keep they alive apathetic and enduring under the cruel persistence of wind and cold while the bestarred black dome of the sky revolve slowly above the ship that drift bear their patience and their suffering through the stormy solitude of the sea
huddle close to one another they fancy themselves utterly alone
they hear sustain loud noise and again bear the pain of existence through long hour of profound silence
in the night they see sunshine feel warmth and suddenly with start think that the sun would never rise upon freeze world
some hear laughter listen to song other near the end of the poop could hear loud human shriek and open their eye be surprised to hear they still though very faint and far away
the boatswain said:—“why it be the cook hail from forward think

the man sigh out part with the mug:—“how as he do it
some cry weakly:—“bully for you doctor
he have do it somehow
afterwards archie declare that the thing be meeraculous
for many day we wonder and it be the one ever interesting subject of conversation to the end of the voyage
we ask the cook in fine weather how he feel when he see his stove rear up on end
we inquire in the north east trade and on serene evening whether he have to stand on his head to put thing right somewhat
we suggest he have use his bread board for raft and from there comfortably have stoke his grate and we do our good to conceal our admiration under the wit of fine irony
he affirm not to know anything about it rebuke our levity declare himself with solemn animation to have be the object of special mercy for the saving of our unholy life
fundamentally he be right no doubt but he need not have be so offensively positive about it he need not have hint so often that it would have go hard with we have he not be there meritorious and pure to receive the inspiration and the strength for the work of grace
have we be save by his recklessness or his agility we could have at length become reconcile to the fact but to admit our obligation to anybody 's virtue and holiness alone be as difficult for we as for any other handful of mankind
